title: Towing Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Towing Business: Real Costs, Earnings, and Steps
metaDescription: Honest guide to starting a towing business: real truck and licensing costs, realistic monthly earnings, how to win accounts, and what most operators get wrong.
bestFor: People who can handle on-call night and weekend work, capital for a truck, and the heavy regulatory side of hauling vehicles
biggestRisk: Buying or financing a truck before securing steady accounts, then drowning in payments while waiting for call volume
whatItIs: A towing business moves disabled, illegally parked, repossessed, or wrecked vehicles using flatbed or wheel-lift tow trucks. This is light- and medium-duty towing, passenger cars, pickups, vans, and light commercial vehicles, as opposed to heavy-duty recovery of semis and buses, which needs far larger and more expensive rigs. Revenue comes from several streams: private calls and breakdowns, contracts with motor clubs and insurers, repossession work for lenders, rotation lists with police departments for accident and abandoned-vehicle calls, and impound or property-management towing. It is a regulated, capital-heavy automotive service, not a casual side gig: trucks are expensive, commercial insurance is high, and most jurisdictions require specific permits, DOT compliance, and consumer-protection rules around fees and storage.
dailyReality: Most of the work is reactive and dispatched. You wait for calls, from a motor club app, a dispatcher, a lender's repo platform, or directly from stranded drivers, then drive to the vehicle, secure it correctly to a flatbed or with a wheel lift, and deliver it to a shop, home, impound lot, or auction. A single tow takes 20 to 60 minutes on scene plus drive time, and accident scenes can be stressful, messy, and require working alongside police. Demand is heaviest at night, in bad weather, and on weekends, so on-call hours are the norm and a solo operator's sleep gets interrupted. Around the driving you handle invoicing, motor-club payment portals, lien and storage paperwork for abandoned vehicles, fuel, and constant truck maintenance.

startupCostsItemized.item: Used light/medium-duty tow truck (flatbed or wheel-lift)
Straps, chains, dollies, wheel skates, safety lighting
USDOT number, MC authority, state tow permits
LLC, business license, bonding where required

realEarnings.yearOne: A new solo operator with one truck typically nets $4,000 to $8,000 per month in year one after fuel, insurance, and a truck payment, and often less in the first few months while call volume builds. Gross revenue per truck commonly runs $12,000 to $25,000 monthly, but insurance, financing, and fuel eat a large share.
realEarnings.experienced: Established operators with two to four trucks, a police rotation spot or steady lender/motor-club contracts, and a paid storage lot commonly net $8,000 to $18,000 per month per owner. Impound storage fees and repossession volume are where steady operators make their margin.
realEarnings.topEarners: Multi-truck fleets with heavy-duty recovery capability, multiple municipal contracts, and a busy impound yard gross hundreds of thousands per month, with owners netting $200,000 to $600,000+ per year. Getting there means a fleet of expensive trucks, drivers on payroll around the clock, political relationships for rotation lists, and tight compliance, it is a logistics company, not a one-truck hustle.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Securing recurring volume, police rotation, lender repo contracts, motor-club agreements, matters far more than your truck. Storage and impound fees, fuel efficiency, and keeping the truck running without costly downtime are the next biggest levers.
realEarnings.perHour: Counting on-call downtime, a solo operator's effective rate is modest, often $25 to $60 per hour across all hours committed. Active towing pays well per job ($75 to $300+ per tow), but you are paid for completed calls, not the hours you spend available.

howToStart: Month 1: Decide your niche, private/motor-club towing, repossession, or impound/police rotation, because each has different trucks, permits, and contracts. Form an LLC, and price out the real cost of commercial on-hook and garage-keepers insurance before buying anything; it can be a deal-breaker.

Month 2: Get your USDOT number and any state tow operator permits, then buy a reliable used flatbed or wheel-lift truck. Have a heavy-truck mechanic inspect it first, a cheap truck with a failing transmission will sink you.

Month 3: Sign up with motor clubs (Agero, Allstate/Roadside, AAA where available) and repo forwarding companies to get call volume immediately, even at lower per-tow rates, while you build direct relationships with local shops and lenders.

Months 3-6: Apply to your municipality's police rotation list (often a waitlist and inspection), line up a storage lot, and standardize your invoicing and lien paperwork so abandoned-vehicle and storage revenue is captured legally.

Months 6-12: Track which call sources actually pay well and on time; drop unprofitable motor-club work as direct and storage revenue grows, and only add a second truck once one truck is consistently booked.

mustHave: A valid driver's license (and CDL where the truck or load requires it) plus a clean enough record to be insurable
Mechanical confidence to safely secure, load, and tow vehicles without damaging them
Tolerance for unpredictable on-call hours, night work, and high-stress accident scenes
canLearn: Proper rigging and recovery technique for different vehicles and situations
Motor-club, repo-platform, and police-rotation procedures and paperwork
Lien, storage, and abandoned-vehicle law in your state
separators: Locking in recurring contracts (lenders, police rotation, fleets) instead of relying on one-off calls
Running a profitable storage/impound lot where fees compound while a vehicle sits
Cost control on insurance, fuel, and truck maintenance so each truck stays cash-positive

whatPeopleGetWrong: Financing an expensive truck before having any committed call volume, then missing payments while volume slowly builds
Underestimating commercial towing insurance, which is one of the highest premiums in any small business
Treating low-paying motor-club calls as the whole business instead of a starter while building direct and storage revenue
Damaging vehicles through poor rigging and getting hit with claims that their garage-keepers coverage may not fully absorb
Ignoring state consumer-protection rules on towing fees, notice, and storage, which trigger fines and lost rotation privileges
Burning out as a solo operator by trying to cover 24/7 calls alone with no backup driver

toolsAndEquipment.note: The versatile workhorse for most light-duty work; safer for AWD and low cars than a hook.
Cheaper and nimble in tight spots; common for repo and parking enforcement.
Replace worn straps regularly; failure here causes damage claims and danger.
For moving stuck or all-wheel-drive vehicles without dragging them.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Amber safety lighting and traffic cones
toolsAndEquipment.note: Often legally required and essential for roadside safety.
How most calls reach you early on; some are subscription-based.

methods: Signing onto motor-club and roadside networks (Agero, Honk, AAA contractors) for instant call volume while you build other channels
Repossession forwarding companies and direct relationships with local banks, credit unions, and buy-here-pay-here lots
Getting onto the local police or sheriff rotation list for accident and abandoned-vehicle calls
Direct accounts with repair shops, dealerships, fleets, and property managers who need regular towing
A Google Business Profile and 24-hour phone presence so stranded drivers searching 'tow truck near me' reach you
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Lenders needing repossessions, motor clubs and insurers needing roadside fulfillment, police departments needing rotation coverage, repair shops and dealers needing vehicle transport, and property owners needing illegally parked cars removed.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: Motor-club calls can start within weeks of signing up, but profitable direct and contract work takes months to a year to assemble. Police rotation lists often have waitlists, so plan for a slow ramp.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Broad consumer advertising and flashy branding early on convert poorly, towing demand is urgent and contract-driven, so your energy is better spent on rotation lists, lender contracts, and motor-club fulfillment.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes, but it is full-time and capital-intensive from the start, there is no realistic part-time version. A single well-utilized truck on good contracts can produce a full-time income, capped mainly by your availability and the territory's call volume.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: This is the natural growth path: add trucks and drivers to cover more hours and contracts so you stop driving every call. It requires payroll, careful driver vetting (insurance costs hinge on driving records), and 24/7 dispatch coordination.
howItScales.sellable: Towing companies with municipal contracts, an established impound yard, fleet accounts, and clean compliance records sell at a meaningful multiple, often including the real estate and trucks. A single uncontracted owner-operator truck is harder to sell beyond the equipment value.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: More trucks and insured drivers, a secured storage/impound lot, dispatch systems, and durable contracts (police rotation, lenders, fleets). The leap is operational and regulatory, and driver turnover plus insurance are the constant constraints.

strongFit: You have or can finance a reliable truck and respect the heavy insurance and compliance side
You can tolerate on-call nights, weekends, and bad-weather work
You are comfortable building relationships with lenders, police, shops, and motor clubs
You want a defensible, sellable business with recurring contract revenue
poorFit: You want low startup cost or a fast, easy path to income
You cannot commit to unpredictable, around-the-clock availability
You have a driving record or credit that makes commercial insurance or financing unaffordable
You dislike paperwork, regulation, and dealing with police and lien procedures
askYourself: Can I realistically cover or staff 24/7 call availability without burning out?
Have I gotten a real commercial insurance quote, and does the math still work after that premium?
Do I have, or can I win, recurring volume, rotation, lender, or fleet contracts, rather than hoping for random calls?

faqs.question: Do I need a CDL to drive a tow truck?
faqs.answer: It depends on the truck's gross vehicle weight rating and the combined weight of the truck plus the towed vehicle. Many light-duty rollbacks fall under the CDL threshold, but medium- and heavy-duty rigs typically require one. Check your state's specific weight rules and tow-operator endorsements before buying a truck.

faqs.question: How is towing different from roadside assistance?
faqs.answer: Roadside assistance handles fixes that get a driver moving again, jump starts, tire changes, lockouts, fuel delivery, usually with a regular service van. Towing physically hauls a vehicle that cannot be driven, which requires an expensive specialized truck, far higher insurance, and more regulation. Many operators do both, but towing is the heavier, more capital-intensive side.

faqs.question: How much does a tow truck cost?
faqs.answer: A serviceable used light-duty rollback or wheel-lift typically runs $20,000 to $70,000, while new trucks start around $80,000 and climb fast for medium- and heavy-duty units. Have any used truck inspected by a heavy-truck mechanic; a hidden transmission or hydraulics problem can cost more than the truck.

faqs.question: Why is towing insurance so expensive?
faqs.answer: You are driving a heavy truck in stressful conditions and taking custody of other people's vehicles, so insurers price in both commercial auto liability and on-hook/garage-keepers coverage for the vehicles you carry and store. Premiums of $8,000 to $25,000+ per truck per year are common and depend heavily on driving records, so get real quotes before committing.

faqs.question: How do I get on a police rotation list?
faqs.answer: Police and sheriff departments keep rotation lists of approved towers they call for accidents and abandoned vehicles. You usually apply, pass a truck and lot inspection, prove insurance and compliance, and then wait, many lists are capped or have waitlists. Rotation work is steady and valuable, which is exactly why it is competitive.

faqs.question: Is repossession towing a good niche to start in?
faqs.answer: Repo work provides steady, contracted volume from lenders and repo forwarding companies, and wheel-lift trucks suited to it are cheaper than full flatbeds. It also carries extra legal and safety considerations and emotionally charged encounters. It is a legitimate path in, but learn your state's repossession laws carefully before taking jobs.

faqs.question: Can I run a towing business with just one truck?
faqs.answer: Yes, many operators start and stay as a single-truck owner-operator, and a well-booked truck on good contracts can earn a solid full-time income. The trade-off is being tied to the phone around the clock, since one truck and one driver cannot cover 24/7 demand without burning out.

dataSources: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, occupational and wage data for tow truck operators and automotive service
Towing and Recovery Association of America (TRAA) industry guidance and operator standards
IBISWorld, Towing Services in the US industry overview (revenue and margin trends)
Commercial insurance broker cost guides for tow operators (on-hook and garage-keepers coverage)
Operator communities (TowForce, r/Towing) for real-world pricing, contracts, and earnings
